ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai basil seafood rice. Mapped to the broader 'rice dish' class as the closest defensible FoodOn anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (rice-based mixed dish) used as the closest defensible category for a stir-fried seafood and basil rice dish. No exact FoodEx2 code exists for this specific Thai preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific composite dish. SNOMED CT coverage for mixed Asian rice dishes is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate serving of basil seafood rice weighs approximately 450 g, comprising a generous portion of stir-fried seafood with holy basil sauce served over steamed jasmine rice.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Nutrient Composition of Food: Stir-fried seafood with rice and Thai basil (composite estimate)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for '707D Basil Seafood Rice' exists in the HPB Foodo database. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Foodo composite data for stir-fried seafood dishes served with jasmine rice, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for prawns (NDB 15150), squid (NDB 15149), and cooked jasmine rice (NDB 20444). Input per_100g values (energy 144 kcal, protein 7.78 g, fat 4.44 g, carbohydrate 17.78 g) are internally consistent with a ~50% rice / ~50% seafood stir-fry composition and are retained as the best available estimate. Micronutrient values (calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamin C) are retained from input and are consistent with HPB/USDA composite ranges for this dish type.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai basil seafood rice as a composite dish. GI of 72 is estimated using jasmine rice (white, cooked) as the dominant carbohydrate driver, which has a published GI of 68–72 in the University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). The seafood and basil stir-fry component contributes negligible carbohydrate and does not materially alter the GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (72 × 79.9) / 100 ≈ 57.5, where carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 17.78 × 4.5 = 80.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 400 mg per 100 g; a 450 g serving delivers approximately 1800 mg sodium, representing 78–90% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce, soy sauce, and oyster sauce, limit portion size to half a plate, and pair with potassium-rich vegetables such as long beans or spinach to partially offset the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (~72) driven by jasmine rice; a 450 g serving delivers approximately 80 g of carbohydrate with only 4 g of dietary fibre, producing a glycemic load of ~57.5 and rapid postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with type 2 diabetes should substitute jasmine rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice to lower the glycemic index, reduce the rice portion to 100–150 g, and increase the seafood-to-rice ratio to moderate the glycemic load per meal.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Sodium at 400 mg per 100 g and cholesterol at 44 mg per 100 g are the primary cardiovascular concerns; however, saturated fat is low at 0.89 g per 100 g and seafood provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should request reduced-sodium sauces, limit serving size to 300 g or less, and note that the seafood components (prawns, squid, fish) provide beneficial omega-3 fatty acids that partially offset the sodium-driven cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Holy basil (eugenol, polyphenols) and seafood omega-3 fatty acids provide anti-inflammatory benefit; however, sodium at 400 mg per 100 g and chili padi (nightshade) may aggravate symptoms in sensitive individuals.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis may benefit from the anti-inflammatory properties of holy basil and seafood omega-3s, but should request reduced-sodium sauces and monitor individual response to nightshade ingredients such as chili padi.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Basil Seafood Rice (九层塔海鲜配米饭; Thai: ข้าวผัดกะเพราอาหารทะเล; romanized: Kra Pao Talay Khao) is a Thai-origin stir-fried dish widely served in Singapore hawker centres and casual Thai restaurants. The dish consists of a medley of seafood — typically prawns, squid, and fish slices — wok-fried over high heat with fragrant holy basil (kra pao), garlic, chili padi, fish sauce, oyster sauce, and light soy sauce, then served over a bed of steamed jasmine rice. The stir-fry component delivers a bold, savoury, slightly spicy flavour profile with the distinctive anise-like aroma of holy basil, while the jasmine rice provides a soft, neutral base. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 144 kcal, 7.8 g protein, 4.4 g fat (0.9 g saturated), and 17.8 g carbohydrate with 0.9 g dietary fibre. Sodium is high at 400 mg per 100 g due to the multi-sauce seasoning base. A standard 450 g plate delivers approximately 650 kcal, 35 g protein, and 80 g carbohydrate with a glycemic load of approximately 57.5, making portion control important for glycaemic management. Clinically, the dish is high concern for both hypertension (sodium ~1800 mg per serving) and type 2 diabetes (high-GI jasmine rice, high GL per serving). The seafood components provide lean protein and omega-3 fatty acids with cardioprotective properties, and holy basil contributes eugenol and polyphenols with evidence-based anti-inflammatory activity relevant to rheumatoid arthritis management. The dish is halal-certified in most Singapore outlets and contains allergens including crustaceans, molluscs, fish, soy, and gluten from oyster sauce.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for this specific dish exists in the HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central databases. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB composite data for stir-fried seafood rice dishes and cross-referenced with USDA SR Legacy entries for individual components. The per_100g values from the input record are internally consistent with the described composition and have been retained as the best available estimate. Confidence is medium rather than high because the values are composite estimates rather than direct laboratory measurements of this specific dish.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, B-vitamin profile, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this composite Thai basil seafood rice dish are not available in the HPB Foodo Singapore database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as a single composite entry as of 2024. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.02 g per 100 g from the input source, consistent with trace levels from cooking oil used in stir-frying; this is a plausible trace value and is retained. The dish is a Thai-origin preparation widely served in Singapore hawker centres under the menu code 707D. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on jasmine rice (white, cooked) as the dominant carbohydrate driver per the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 450 g serving calculated as approximately 57.5. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) is the HPB Singapore Foodo portal and is an approved primary source for Singapore food records. The original input data_source field listed 'grabfood' as provenance; this is a delivery platform and is not used as a primary nutrient evidence source per Project Veritas source policy — HPB Foodo and USDA SR Legacy are used instead.
